task:
You are Claude Code acting as a **performance-obsessed build systems engineer** for a **Next.js + Turborepo** monorepo. Your job: make **builds, lints, and typechecks insanely fast** so an agent can iterate rapidly.

You MUST:
- Prefer **latest stable versions** of tooling (Node, Next.js, Turborepo, TypeScript, lint tools, package manager).
- If you’re unsure what “latest stable” is, **look it up in official docs/release notes** and write down exact versions you will target.
- Implement changes as concrete file edits + diffs.

## Primary references you should use (official docs)
- Turborepo Remote Caching: https://turborepo.dev/docs/core-concepts/remote-caching
- Next.js caching guide (App Router): https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/caching
- Next.js CI build caching (.next/cache): https://nextjs.org/docs/pages/guides/ci-build-caching
- TypeScript Project References (tsc --build): https://www.typescriptlang.org/docs/handbook/project-references.html
- TypeScript performance notes (incremental builds, etc.): https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/wiki/Performance
- ESLint CLI cache flags: https://eslint.org/docs/latest/use/command-line-interface
- pnpm CI guidance: https://pnpm.io/continuous-integration
- Biome CLI reference: https://biomejs.dev/reference/cli/
- Biome CI recipe (biome ci vs check): https://biomejs.dev/recipes/continuous-integration/
- Biome VCS integration (Git-aware features): https://biomejs.dev/guides/integrate-in-vcs/

(Use those links to confirm flags/paths/features instead of guessing.)

## Non-negotiables (speed + correctness)
- Maximize caching (local + remote), minimize duplicated work, keep correctness.
- No vague advice. Every recommendation must map to:
  - exact file(s) changed
  - exact config changes
  - exact command(s)

## Step 0: Repo audit (do this first)
Inspect and summarize:
- package manager + lockfile (pnpm/yarn/npm)
- node version strategy (engines, .nvmrc, volta, etc.)
- Next.js version(s), TypeScript version, tsconfig struc

This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Analysis:
Let me go through the conversation chronologically to capture all important details.

1. The user's request was a comprehensive build performance optimization task for a Next.js + Turborepo monorepo. They wanted to act as a "performance-obsessed build systems engineer" and make builds, lints, and typechecks extremely fast.

2. The user provided detailed instructions including:
   - Step 0: Repo audit
   - Execution checklist with 5 sections (Turborepo caching, Next.js, TypeScript, Linting, CI)
   - Specific output format required
   - Non-negotiables about caching and concrete changes

3. My approach:
   - First did a comprehensive audit reading many config files
   - Identified 3 major bottlenecks
   - Created a prioritized plan
   - Implemented changes in order of impact

4. Key findings from audit:
   - pnpm 10.14.0, Node 22, Turbo 2.7.3, Next.js 16.1.1, TS 5.9.3
   - ~202 workspace packages (3 apps + 15 core + 184 tools)
   - TypeScript incremental was DISABLED in all 195 package tsconfigs (overriding base)
   - Dual lint system: Biome 2.3.11 + ESLint 9.39.2
   - turbo.json had `**/.env.*local` in globalDependencies (too broad)
   - type-check depended on own build unnecessarily: `["build", "^build"]`
   - CI had 5 separate jobs each running pnpm install, no remote cache
   - lint job ran full `pnpm build` before linting (redundant)

5. Changes made:

   a) **turbo.json** changes:
      - Removed `**/.env.*local` from globalDependencies (narrowed to just `tsconfig.json`)
      - Changed type-check dependsOn from `["build", "^build"]` to `["^build"]`
      - Added outputs for type-check: `["dist/**/*.tsbuildinfo", "*.tsbuildinfo", ".next/cache/tsconfig.tsbuildinfo"]`
      - Added explicit `outputs: []` for lint

   b) **packages/config/tsconfig/base.json** changes:
      - Changed `composite: true` to
